content:
Hi!

I was wondering whether it's supported to build Rust libraries in the kernel that are then consumed by C code. I'm personally eyeing x86 emulation code in KVM as a good target to move into a Rust library (because it's prime attack surface). Is there any chance that something like this might be upstreamed? Or is calling Rust from C code frowned upon in the RfL project?

Thanks, Julian

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

There's a third version of the patch, so if you have any though about it go and speak it up!

Here I have a patch that builds your Rust crates outside of kernel wherever you like it. If you want to call those functions from C code you'd add extern "C" to them so they can be called from. You can add those function definitions to a header and you'll have C bindings to a Rust library.
I'll send a fourth version of the patch which contains a dependency fix that was requested, so stay tuned.

Julian Stecklina said:

Is there any chance that something like this might be upstreamed?

Possibly -- it would depend on the KVM x86 maintainers and the overall KVM ones (and perhaps x86 too). From a quick look at the lists of maintainers, I think at least two are generally supportive of Rust, so that is nice (though, of course, they may not have time, may not agree with the particular project, etc.).

In case you have not seen it, https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-new-abstractions-and-modules has some general guidelines on how to proceed.

Julian Stecklina said:

I was wondering whether it's supported to build Rust libraries in the kernel that are then consumed by C code. (...) Or is calling Rust from C code frowned upon in the RfL project?

It is definitely something we would love to see happen (and some maintainers have been asking about it!). However, in the beginning, most likely any such effort will need to be opt-in/parallel (i.e. it could potentially be upstreamed, but most likely would be under a config gate nowadays) due to the current constraints (like the version and platform support) to avoid requiring Rust for every user. It would also be nice to provide some facilities to help with that too.

But if you want to experiment for the moment, then you can already start by putting your code in the kernel crate, manually writing the declarations on the C side and compile KVM as built-in (if that does not work for some reason, please feel free to send me an email and I am happy to help).

In fact, if you get that project rolling (and especially if you manage to show its value to the maintainers), it could serve as a justification for introducing the extra support needed to do things properly. So please let us know if you do start working on it!
Thanks for the reply. I'll let you know! Building this code into the kernel has the downside of slower turnaround times for development but could be a way to start and prototype.

When calling rust from C, we met one problem: some rust data structures are opaque to the c side due to the mismatch ABI. That structure's operations must be manually written in separate functions just like the wasi format. This makes the interface between Rust and C very complex. I wonder if any tools can help generate methods for opaque types.
